hub_subtitle: Business English para seguros, reuniões e entrevistas — e o desbloqueio da fala, do primeiro dia ao último

lesson.menu_title: How Much Is It? -- Numbers, Prices and Quantities
lesson.menu_desc: A aula em que o número para de ser inimigo. Ela trabalha com número o dia inteiro — apólice, orçamento, sinistro — e mesmo assim "thirty" e "thirteen" passam voando e ela não pega. Aqui está o segredo que ninguém conta: o americano também não pega. Por isso existe "One-three or three-zero?" — uma frase que o nativo usa porque a língua dele é ambígua, não porque ele é ruim nela. O número é a última coisa que fica automática em qualquer idioma, e isso não é sobre você. Duas perguntas (how much pro que não se conta, how many pro que se conta), some e any, seis palavras de dinheiro (price, cost, budget, available, expensive, bill) — e a virada da aula: no seguro, confirmar um número não é fraqueza — é o trabalho. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- How Much Is It?
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Paula Morales | Lesson 11 -- How Much Is It?
lesson.grammar_point: how much (uncountable/price) vs how many (countable) + quantifiers some (affirmative) and any (negative/question)

listenings.text: Hi, Paula. David here. You have the Miami trip next month, and before that I want to give you the one thing nobody gives you. It is about numbers. You are going to think this is your problem. It is not. Listen. In English, thirteen and thirty are almost the same word. Fifteen and fifty. Sixteen and sixty. One letter of difference in the sound, and it comes at the end, where nobody is listening anymore. Now, here is what I need you to understand: I am American. I was born with this language. And I mishear it too. Every week. In every currency. So we have a trick, and the trick is not a beginner trick, it is what everyone does. We say the digits. One-three, or three-zero? That is it. That is the whole thing. And when you say it, nobody thinks your English is bad, because it is the same question I ask. There is a rule that helps, and it is small. The teens push the sound to the end: thir-TEEN. The tens push it to the front: THIR-ty. If the end is loud, it is a teen. If the front is loud, it is a ten. It works most of the time, and most of the time is enough, because you have the digits for the rest. One more thing, and this one is your job, not your English. You work with numbers all day. You read a policy in Portuguese and you see a wrong figure in two seconds. That does not go away in English. It is slower, that is all. The number is the last thing that becomes automatic in any language. It is the last thing. Not the hardest, the last. See you Monday.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. One thing about Miami, and it is not about English. I have been watching you for three months, and I want to tell you what I see. When a broker gives you a number on the phone, in Portuguese, you repeat it back. Every time. You say the figure out loud and you wait for him to confirm it. I do not think you know you do it. You have done it for twenty years. That is not a habit, Paula, that is the job. In our business a wrong number is not an embarrassment, it is money. It is somebody's factory. So when you get to Miami and the client says a figure and it goes past you, I want you to do exactly what you already do in Portuguese. Say it back. Ask for the digits. Could you repeat that? One-eight-zero? And I want you to notice something while you do it. Nobody at this company has ever lost an account for confirming a figure. Not one. People lose accounts for guessing. For nodding. For writing down eighty when the man said eighteen and finding out in March. So here is the thing I really called to say. You think asking about a number makes you look like a beginner. In this business it makes you look like a professional, and the ones who do not ask are the ones I worry about. You are not going to Miami to prove your English. You are going to get the numbers right. You already know how to do that. See you Monday.

items.situation: You are in the airport shop. You want the blue one, and there is no price on it. Ask.
tips: Excuse me, how much is the blue one?

items.situation: The clerk says a figure and it goes past you. It sounded like fifty, but it could be fifteen. Do not guess -- ask for the digits.
A broker asks how many people are on your team. You have six. Answer with a full sentence, not the number alone.
tips: There are six people on my team.
I have six people on my team, and I handle the large risks.

items.situation: The client asks if you have time on Friday. You do not -- Friday is full. Say it, and this one needs any.
tips: I don't have any time on Friday.
I don't have any meetings on Thursday. Is Thursday possible?

items.situation: You want to know the price of the policy for the client's factory. This is a client, not a colleague -- use the polite one from lesson 9.
tips: Could you tell me the price?
Could you send me the budget for the factory?

items.situation: The hotel bill has a charge you do not recognize. Eighteen dollars, and you did not use the minibar. Ask about it -- and do not say sorry first.
tips: I have a question about the bill. What is the eighteen dollars?

bank.label: Asking the price / Perguntar o preço
items: Could you tell me the price?

bank.label: Quantity, and getting the number right / Quantidade — e acertar o número
items: How many people are on your team?
I have some questions about the budget.
I don't have any time on Friday.
